decisions.question: No design doc found for this branch. /office-hours produces a structured problem statement, premise challenge, and explored alternatives — it gives this review much sharper input to work with. Takes about 10 minutes. The design doc is per-feature, not per-product — it captures the thinking behind this specific change. Would you like to run /office-hours first?

options.label: A) Run /office-hours now (Recommended)
options.description: We'll run /office-hours inline to produce a design doc for Phase 4b, then pick up the CEO review right after. Sharper input = sharper review.

options.label: B) Skip — proceed with standard review
options.description: Proceed with the CEO review using the existing CLAUDE.md, TODOS.md, and prior design doc as context. No structured problem statement.

decisions.chosen: B) Skip — proceed with standard review
decisions.rationale: We already have extensive design docs, CLAUDE.md, TODOS.md, and prior phase design docs that provide sufficient context — spending 10 minutes on /office-hours adds a review cycle before the actual review, and Phase 4b's scope (cron triggers, config hot-reload) is well-understood from prior planning.

decisions.question:
We're reviewing Phase 4b for GaryClaw (the daemon scheduling phase) on the main branch. The plan adds cron triggers and config hot-reload to the daemon. I've identified three implementation approaches:

Approach A is the basic version — add cron triggers using node-cron and SIGHUP for config reload. Simple, small diff, but doesn't handle laptop sleep/wake (missed cron windows are silently lost).

Approach B is the laptop-aware version — cron with catch-up semantics (if the laptop was asleep during a scheduled run, it fires when it wakes up) plus file-watch config reload (works cross-platform, no signal needed). More robust but more complex.

Approach C skips cron entirely and only ships config hot-reload. Users who want scheduling can use system crontab + the existing `garyclaw daemon trigger` command.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose B because GaryClaw runs on developer laptops, not servers. Cron without catch-up is actively wrong in this context — you'd schedule a nightly QA run and it would silently never fire because your laptop was asleep. The extra complexity (~15 min CC time) is the difference between "works" and "works correctly."

Completeness: A=6/10, B=9/10, C=4/10

options.label: A) Minimal Cron + SIGHUP
options.description: Basic cron + signal-based config reload. Small diff, but no laptop sleep awareness. (human: ~3 days / CC: ~30 min)

options.label: B) Smart Scheduling + File Watch (Recommended)
options.description: Laptop-aware cron with catch-up + cross-platform config reload via file watcher. (human: ~1 week / CC: ~45 min)

options.label: C) Config Reload Only
options.description: Skip cron entirely. Ship only config hot-reload. Users use system crontab for scheduling. (human: ~1 day / CC: ~15 min)

decisions.rationale: GaryClaw targets developer laptops where sleep/wake is the norm, so cron without catch-up is silently broken — B is the only option that actually works correctly in the target environment, and 45 min CC time is well within the 1-day auto-approve threshold.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw Phase 4b review on main branch. Approach B (smart scheduling + file watch) is locked in. Now choosing the review mode — this controls how ambitious we get.

Context: This is a feature enhancement to an existing system (adding scheduling to the daemon). The daemon architecture is solid (Phase 4a shipped clean). The scope is well-defined: cron triggers + config hot-reload. I'd default to SELECTIVE EXPANSION — make the plan bulletproof, but surface any interesting opportunities for you to cherry-pick.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose B (Selective Expansion) — the daemon architecture is proven and this is a natural extension. Hold scope as baseline but let me surface opportunities like resource-aware scheduling, quiet hours, or battery-level gating if they're cheap enough to be worth doing.

Completeness: A=10/10 (everything), B=9/10 (right balance), C=7/10 (safe), D=5/10 (minimal)

options.label: A) SCOPE EXPANSION
options.description: Dream big. Push scope UP. Cathedral mode. Every expansion presented for your approval.

options.label: B) SELECTIVE EXPANSION (Recommended)
options.description: Hold scope as baseline. Surface expansion opportunities individually for cherry-picking. Neutral recommendations.

options.label: C) HOLD SCOPE
options.description: Make Phase 4b bulletproof. No expansions surfaced. Maximum rigor on the existing plan.

options.label: D) SCOPE REDUCTION
options.description: Strip to essentials. Find the minimum viable scheduling that ships value.

decisions.rationale: The daemon architecture is proven from Phase 4a, so the baseline scope is safe to hold while surfacing cheap expansions like battery-level gating that fit the laptop-aware theme we already committed to — this matches the prior decision's logic without open-ending the scope.

decisions.question: GaryClaw Phase 4b, Selective Expansion cherry-pick #1. QUIET HOURS: Add a config setting like `quietHours: { start: "22:00", end: "07:00" }` that suppresses all cron triggers during those hours. If a trigger fires during quiet hours, it gets deferred to the end of the quiet window. ~20 LOC in the cron poller + ~5 LOC in config validation. Effort: XS. Risk: Low. Prevents surprise 2am notifications when a nightly cron fires and the laptop happens to be awake.

options.label: A) Add to scope
options.description: Add quiet hours to Phase 4b. ~20 LOC, prevents late-night triggers.

options.label: B) Defer to TODOS.md
options.description: Good idea, but not essential for MVP. Add later.

options.label: C) Skip
options.description: Not worth the complexity.

decisions.rationale: ~20 LOC at XS effort is well within blast radius, covers a real edge case (2am surprise notifications), and aligns with the Approach B 'works correctly on laptops' thesis we already committed to.

decisions.question: GaryClaw Phase 4b, cherry-pick #2. CATCH-UP LIMIT: When the laptop wakes from sleep, the cron poller checks how many windows were missed. Without a limit, a trigger set to every hour that was asleep for 48 hours would fire 48 times at once. The catch-up limit caps it at 1 fire per trigger on wake-up, regardless of how many windows were missed. ~10 LOC in the cron poller. Effort: XS. Risk: Low. This is arguably a bug fix, not an expansion — unbounded catch-up is a thundering herd problem.

options.label: A) Add to scope
options.description: Cap catch-up at 1 per trigger. Prevents thundering herd on laptop wake.

options.label: B) Defer to TODOS.md
options.description: Track as a known edge case for later.

options.label: C) Skip
options.description: Not a real problem in practice.

decisions.rationale: Unbounded catch-up is a bug, not a feature — 10 LOC to prevent a thundering herd is squarely in blast radius and trivial effort.

decisions.question: GaryClaw Phase 4b, cherry-pick #3. CONFIG VALIDATION ON RELOAD: When the file watcher detects a change to daemon.json, validate the new config before applying it. If the new config is invalid (bad cron expression, missing fields, etc.), keep running with the old config and log a warning + send a notification. Without this, a typo in the config file crashes the daemon. ~15 LOC. Effort: XS. Risk: Low. This is a robustness fundamental — production daemons should never crash on config edit.

options.label: A) Add to scope
options.description: Validate before applying. Invalid config = keep old + warn. ~15 LOC.

options.label: B) Defer to TODOS.md
options.description: Track for later hardening.

options.label: C) Skip
options.description: Not worth it — users should test their config.

decisions.rationale: A daemon that crashes on a config typo is a bug, not a missing feature — this is squarely in the blast radius of config hot-reload and costs 15 LOC.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw Phase 4b, cherry-pick #4. DAEMON SCHEDULE CLI: Add a `garyclaw daemon schedule` subcommand that shows all configured cron triggers with their next fire time and countdown. Like a friendlier `crontab -l`. Example output:

```
Trigger  | Schedule      | Skills           | Next Run
cron-1   | 0 3 * * *     | qa               | in 4h 23m
cron-2   | 0 9 * * MON   | design-review    | in 2d 8h
git-poll | every 60s     | qa, ship         | (on push)
```

~40 LOC (IPC request type + CLI display). Effort: S. Risk: Low. Gives users visibility into what the daemon is doing. Maps to the engineering preference for observability.

options.label: A) Add to scope
options.description: Add `daemon schedule` command showing next fire times. ~40 LOC.

options.label: B) Defer to TODOS.md
options.description: Nice UX but not essential. Add later.

options.label: C) Skip
options.description: Users can read daemon.json directly.

decisions.rationale: 40 LOC for daemon observability is squarely in blast radius of the scheduling feature — users need to verify their cron config is correct, and reading raw JSON is not that.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw Phase 4b on main. Architecture concern: daemon-state.json (which stores job history + lastRunTimestamps) uses a single file write with no rotation. checkpoint.ts already has 2-rotation (write to .tmp, rename, keep previous as .bak) for corruption recovery. If the laptop loses power mid-write to daemon-state.json, the file could be corrupted and all lastRunTimestamps would be lost — causing every cron trigger to fire a catch-up on next daemon start.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose A — reuse the same atomic write pattern from checkpoint.ts. ~10 LOC, eliminates the corruption risk. Maps to the engineering preference for handling more edge cases.

Completeness: A=9/10, B=6/10

options.label: A) Add 2-rotation to daemon-state.json (Recommended)
options.description: Reuse checkpoint.ts atomic write pattern. ~10 LOC. Prevents corruption from power loss mid-write.

options.label: B) Skip — acceptable risk
options.description: Power loss mid-write is rare. Catch-up re-fire is annoying but not destructive.

decisions.rationale: Reusing checkpoint.ts's proven atomic write pattern for ~10 LOC is textbook DRY + completeness — it's in the blast radius, trivial effort, and prevents a real corruption→thundering-herd bug.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw Phase 4b on main. Edge case: DST transitions. When clocks spring forward (2am→3am), a cron trigger scheduled for 2:30am has no valid fire time. When clocks fall back (2am repeats), a trigger at 1:30am could fire twice. cron-parser likely handles this correctly (it's a mature library), but the behavior should be tested. This is a test gap, not an implementation gap.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose A — adding 2-3 DST edge case tests costs minutes with CC and prevents a subtle bug that would only surface twice a year. Classic 'boil the lake' opportunity.

Completeness: A=9/10, B=6/10

options.label: A) Add DST edge case tests (Recommended)
options.description: Test spring-forward (skipped hour) and fall-back (repeated hour) with mock dates. ~3 tests, ~30 LOC.

options.label: B) Skip — trust cron-parser
options.description: cron-parser is mature and likely handles DST. Document as a known assumption.

decisions.chosen: A) Add DST edge case tests (Recommended)
decisions.rationale: A twice-a-year silent bug is exactly the kind of edge case that 30 LOC of tests eliminates permanently, and it's consistent with every prior cherry-pick decision in this phase.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw Phase 4b on main. DRY concern: the 2-rotation atomic write pattern (write to .tmp, rename, keep .bak) currently lives inside checkpoint.ts. You just accepted adding the same pattern to daemon-state.json. Rather than copy-pasting, should we extract a shared `atomicWriteJSON(path, data)` utility that both checkpoint.ts and job-runner.ts use?

RECOMMENDATION: Choose A — it's ~15 LOC for the utility, eliminates duplication, and any future JSON state files get it for free. Maps directly to the DRY engineering preference.

Completeness: A=9/10, B=6/10

options.label: A) Extract shared atomicWriteJSON utility (Recommended)
options.description: New utility (~15 LOC), used by checkpoint.ts + job-runner.ts. DRY.

options.label: B) Copy-paste the pattern
options.description: Duplicate the logic. Faster to write, but two copies to maintain.

decisions.rationale: Textbook DRY — two copies of the same 2-rotation write pattern is exactly the duplication P4 exists to prevent, and a 15-LOC utility pays for itself immediately.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw Phase 4b on main. Long-term trajectory question: quiet hours is currently scoped as a daemon-level config (`DaemonConfig.quietHours?`), but the cron poller is the only consumer. Should git_poll triggers also respect quiet hours? If someone pushes code at 2am, do they want the git poll trigger to auto-run QA and send a notification? Probably not.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose A — quiet hours should apply to ALL triggers, not just cron. It's already at the DaemonConfig level, so the implementation is just adding the `isInQuietHours()` check to the git poller's debounce callback too (~3 LOC). Consistent behavior.

Completeness: A=9/10, B=7/10

options.label: A) Quiet hours applies to all triggers (Recommended)
options.description: Add isInQuietHours() check to git poller too. ~3 LOC. Consistent behavior.

options.label: B) Cron-only quiet hours
options.description: Keep quiet hours scoped to cron triggers. Git poll always fires.

decisions.chosen: A) Quiet hours applies to all triggers (Recommended)
decisions.rationale: Quiet hours is a daemon-level concept and should consistently suppress all trigger types — a 2am git push notification is exactly the kind of thing quiet hours exists to prevent, and it's only ~3 LOC.

decisions.question:
All 10 review sections are complete. Want an outside voice? A different AI system can give a brutally honest, independent challenge of this plan — logical gaps, feasibility risks, and blind spots that are hard to catch from inside the review. Takes about 2 minutes.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose A — an independent second opinion catches structural blind spots. Two different AI models agreeing on a plan is stronger signal than one model's thorough review. Completeness: A=9/10, B=7/10.

options.label: A) Get the outside voice (Recommended)
options.description: Independent Claude subagent reviews the plan with fresh context.

options.label: B) Skip — proceed to outputs
options.description: Skip the independent review. Proceed to final outputs.

decisions.rationale: An independent review catches blind spots cheaply — 2 minutes for a structural integrity check on a plan that drives weeks of work is pure upside.

decisions.question: GaryClaw Phase 4b on main. Cross-model disagreement on catchUpLimit. The review said catchUpLimit guards against future sub-minute poll intervals. The outside voice says it's dead code — the algorithm calls prev() once per tick, so it can NEVER fire more than once. The limit field is impossible to use as designed. Either: (A) remove the field entirely (simpler, honest), (B) fix the algorithm to support it (iterate prev() to find N missed windows, fire up to catchUpLimit). Worth investigating further?

options.label: A) Remove catchUpLimit field
options.description: Delete dead config. Catch-up always fires exactly once. Simpler and honest.

options.label: B) Fix algorithm to support limits > 1
options.description: Iterate prev() to find N missed windows. More complex but the field works as documented.

options.label: C) Add to TODOS.md
options.description: Keep the field for now, document it fires exactly once. Fix later if needed.

decisions.rationale: Dead code is a lie in the codebase — the field promises behavior the algorithm cannot deliver, and removing it is the 10-line obvious fix over building complexity to support a feature nobody has asked for.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw Phase 4b on main. Cross-model disagreement on fs.watch. The review used fs.watch as primary + mtime polling as safety net. The outside voice says mtime polling every 5s IS the real implementation, and fs.watch is unreliable dead weight on macOS. Since the daemon already has a 5s processNext() tick, mtime polling is trivially cheap and reliable. fs.watch's only advantage is sub-second detection, which doesn't matter for config reload (nobody needs config applied in <1s).

RECOMMENDATION: Choose A — drop fs.watch entirely. Simpler code, more reliable, no platform quirks. The 5s mtime poll is sufficient.

Completeness: A=9/10, B=7/10

options.label: A) Drop fs.watch, use mtime-only polling (Recommended)
options.description: Poll mtime on every processNext() tick (5s). Simpler, reliable, no platform quirks.

options.label: B) Keep both (fs.watch primary + mtime fallback)
options.description: fs.watch for sub-second detection, mtime as fallback. More complex but potentially faster.

decisions.chosen: A) Drop fs.watch, use mtime-only polling (Recommended)
decisions.rationale: fs.watch adds platform-specific complexity for a sub-second advantage nobody needs — mtime polling on the existing 5s tick is simpler, reliable, and sufficient.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw Phase 4b on main. TODO proposal #1: TRIGGER KEY CLEANUP. When a cron trigger config is edited (expression changes), the old trigger key becomes orphaned in daemon-state.json's lastRunTimestamps. Over months of config edits, this accumulates stale entries. Fix: on config reload, prune lastRunTimestamps keys that don't match any current trigger.

Effort: XS (human: ~2 hours / CC: ~10 min)
Priority: P3
Depends on: Phase 4b (cron triggers)

Pros: Prevents state file bloat. Clean state.
Cons: Very minor issue — stale keys are harmless except for file size.

options.label: A) Add to TODOS.md
options.description: Track trigger key cleanup as P3 deferred work.

options.label: B) Skip — not valuable
options.description: Stale keys are harmless. Not worth tracking.

options.label: C) Build it now in Phase 4b
options.description: Add pruning logic to config reload. ~10 LOC.

decisions.chosen: C) Build it now in Phase 4b
decisions.rationale: At ~10 LOC and ~10 min CC effort, this is squarely in the blast radius of the cron trigger work and trivially cheap to ship now rather than defer.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw Phase 4b on main. TODO proposal #2: DIFF-BASED CONFIG RELOAD. Currently the plan restarts ALL pollers on any config change, even if only a budget number changed. This resets git poller baselines (loses current HEAD), causing a brief window where a push could be missed. Fix: compare old and new configs, only restart triggers that actually changed.

Effort: S (human: ~3 days / CC: ~20 min)
Priority: P2
Depends on: Phase 4b

Pros: No missed git events on config edit. Surgical reload.
Cons: Diff logic adds complexity. Must define what 'changed' means for each trigger type.

options.label: A) Add to TODOS.md
options.description: Track diff-based reload as P2 follow-up work.

options.label: B) Skip — not valuable
options.description: Missing one git event during config reload is acceptable.

options.label: C) Build it now in Phase 4b
options.description: Add diff-based reload. ~40 LOC extra.

decisions.chosen: C) Build it now in Phase 4b
decisions.rationale: ~20 min CC effort is well under the 1-day threshold, it's squarely in the blast radius of config reload, and losing git poller baselines is a real correctness bug — not just cosmetic.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw Phase 4b on main. TODO proposal #3: TIMEZONE-AWARE CRON. When the laptop's timezone changes (travel), cron expressions evaluate differently — a 3am EST trigger becomes 3am PST, which is 6am EST. This can cause unexpected catch-up fires or missed windows. Fix: store the intended timezone alongside the cron expression in the CronTrigger config, and pass it to cron-parser (which supports `tz` option).

Effort: XS (human: ~2 hours / CC: ~10 min)
Priority: P2
Depends on: Phase 4b

Pros: Correct behavior for traveling developers. cron-parser has native TZ support.
Cons: Adds a config field. Most users won't notice the issue.

options.label: A) Add to TODOS.md
options.description: Track timezone-aware cron as P2 future work.

options.label: B) Skip — edge case too rare
options.description: Most devs don't change timezones frequently enough to matter.

options.label: C) Build it now in Phase 4b
options.description: Add optional `timezone` field to CronTrigger. ~5 LOC + tests.

decisions.chosen: C) Build it now in Phase 4b
decisions.rationale: XS effort, cron-parser already supports the tz option natively, and it's squarely in the blast radius of Phase 4b cron work — ~5 LOC to do it right now vs. reopening later.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw Phase 4b on main. TODO proposal #4: PIN CRON-PARSER VERSION. The outside voice flagged that cron-parser has had breaking API changes between major versions (v4 changed the surface). The plan uses `prev()` and `next()` which are core API — pinning the exact version in package.json prevents surprise breakage on `npm update`.

RECOMMENDATION: This is a no-brainer — just pin the version in package.json. Not really a TODO, more of an implementation detail. I'll note it as required in the plan and move on.

options.label: A) Pin version (just do it)
options.description: Pin exact cron-parser version in package.json. Standard practice.

options.label: B) Skip — use caret range
options.description: Use ^x.y.z and trust semver.

decisions.rationale: Pinning an exact version is a 5-second change that prevents a known class of breakage — pure upside, zero cost.

decisions.question:
CEO review for Phase 4b is complete. Next step: this review expanded scope significantly (9 accepted items including diff-based reload, timezone-aware cron, shared atomicWriteJSON utility, quiet hours for all triggers). The existing eng review from 2026-03-25 covered Phase 4a, not these Phase 4b additions. A fresh eng review would validate the architecture decisions (mtime polling, catch-up algorithm, cron-parser dependency) and produce the test specs.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose A — eng review is the required shipping gate and needs to cover the expanded Phase 4b scope. The CEO plan document gives it sharp input to work from.

Completeness: A=9/10, B=7/10, C=5/10

options.label: A) Run /plan-eng-review next (Recommended)
options.description: Required shipping gate. Covers Phase 4b architecture, tests, error paths. ~20 min.

options.label: B) Run /plan-design-review next
options.description: Not recommended — Phase 4b has no UI scope.

options.label: C) Skip — handle reviews manually
options.description: Proceed to implementation without further reviews.

decisions.rationale: Eng review is the natural shipping gate after CEO review, and the expanded Phase 4b scope (mtime polling, catch-up algorithm, diff-based reload, timezone cron) needs architectural validation and test specs before implementation.

decisions.question: GaryClaw Phase 4b on main. No design doc found for this branch. /office-hours produces a structured problem statement, premise challenge, and explored alternatives — it gives this review much sharper input to work with. Takes about 10 minutes. However, the CEO review just completed with a detailed plan document covering scope decisions, design choices, and temporal interrogation. That likely serves as sufficient input. Skip to the eng review?

options.label: Skip — proceed with review (Recommended)
options.description: The CEO plan document is thorough enough to serve as the design input for this eng review. Completeness: 9/10.

options.label: Run /office-hours first
options.description: Produce a formal design doc before reviewing. Adds ~10 min. Completeness: 10/10.

decisions.rationale: The CEO plan document already covers scope, design choices, and alternatives — running /office-hours would duplicate that input for marginal completeness gain.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw Phase 4b on main. ARCHITECTURE ISSUE 1: Who owns DaemonState persistence?

The plan adds lastRunTimestamps to DaemonState and says the cron poller will persist state via atomicWriteJSON() after each tick. But right now, DaemonState is exclusively managed by job-runner.ts — loadState() and persistState() are private functions inside that module (lines 269-291). The cron poller in triggers.ts would need to read AND write the same daemon-state.json file.

This creates a coupling problem: two modules (job-runner + cron poller) writing to the same file, and the cron poller needs access to state it doesn't own.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose A — separate files keeps each module owning its own persistence, which is the current pattern. Minimal diff, no refactoring of job-runner internals. Completeness: A=9/10, B=8/10, C=6/10.

options.label: A) Separate cron-state.json (Recommended)
options.description: Cron poller manages its own state file (lastRunTimestamps only). ~10 LOC. Clean separation of concerns. (human: ~1 hour / CC: ~5 min)

options.label: B) Expose state on JobRunner
options.description: Add getLastRunTimestamps()/setLastRunTimestamp() methods to JobRunner interface. Cron poller writes via runner. Keeps one state file. (human: ~2 hours / CC: ~10 min)

options.label: C) Keep in daemon-state.json as planned
options.description: Have cron poller import and call persistState directly. Two modules writing same file. Single-threaded so no race, but tight coupling. (human: ~30 min / CC: ~5 min)

decisions.rationale: Each module owning its own persistence file matches the existing pattern, avoids coupling two writers to the same file, and is the smallest diff.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw Phase 4b on main. ARCHITECTURE ISSUE 2: How do git pollers respect quiet hours?

The plan says quiet hours suppresses ALL triggers (cron + git_poll). But the current git poller in triggers.ts (line 75-83) fires its callback immediately on debounce — it has no concept of quiet hours. The plan doesn't specify WHERE the quiet hours check goes for git triggers.

Three places it could go:
- Inside createGitPoller (pollutes the generic poller with daemon-specific config)
- In daemon.ts's trigger callback wrapper (the anonymous function on line 277-279 that calls runner.enqueue)
- In the job runner's enqueue() method

RECOMMENDATION: Choose A — the daemon is the config owner and already wraps trigger callbacks. Adding the quiet hours check there keeps the poller and runner generic. One line of code in the right place. Explicit > clever. Completeness: A=9/10, B=7/10, C=7/10.

options.label: A) Check in daemon.ts callback (Recommended)
options.description: Add isInQuietHours() check in the trigger callback wrapper in daemon.ts before calling runner.enqueue(). ~3 LOC. Daemon owns config, so it should gate triggers. (human: ~30 min / CC: ~3 min)

options.label: B) Check in runner.enqueue()
options.description: Job runner checks quiet hours before accepting jobs. Requires passing QuietHours config to runner. Couples runner to scheduling concerns. (human: ~1 hour / CC: ~5 min)

options.label: C) Pass quietHours to createGitPoller
options.description: Git poller suppresses fires during quiet hours internally. Requires changing GitPoller interface + adding quiet hours dependency. (human: ~1 hour / CC: ~8 min)

decisions.rationale: The daemon owns the config and already wraps trigger callbacks, so gating quiet hours there keeps both the poller and runner generic — one check in the right place, ~3 LOC.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw Phase 4b on main. ARCHITECTURE ISSUE 3: Trigger equality for diff-based reload.

The plan says diff-based config reload only restarts triggers that actually changed. But it doesn't define how to compare two triggers for equality. For git_poll triggers, if you change ONLY debounceSeconds (30→60), should the poller restart?

This matters because restarting a git poller resets its baseline HEAD — there's a brief window where a push could be missed. But NOT restarting means the old config stays in effect.

The simplest approach: JSON.stringify() comparison of the full trigger object. If any field differs, restart. This is correct behavior (old config should not persist) and the "missed push" window is <5s (one poll interval) which is negligible.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose A — simple deep-compare via JSON.stringify is correct, explicit, and covers all trigger types (git_poll + cron) without type-specific comparison logic. Completeness: A=9/10, B=8/10.

options.label: A) JSON.stringify comparison (Recommended)
options.description: Compare triggers by serialized form. Any field change triggers restart. Simple, correct, no type-specific logic. (human: ~30 min / CC: ~3 min)

options.label: B) Field-by-field comparison per type
options.description: Compare specific fields per trigger type (type, expression, skills, interval). More explicit but more code, and must be updated when adding new trigger types. (human: ~2 hours / CC: ~10 min)

decisions.rationale: Simple deep-compare is correct, covers all trigger types uniformly, and avoids maintenance burden when new trigger types are added — 10-line obvious fix beats type-specific abstraction.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw Phase 4b on main. ARCHITECTURE ISSUE 4: Poller interface naming.

The current interface is `GitPoller` with `start()`/`stop()`. The new cron poller has the same shape. The plan implies creating `createCronPoller()` returning... what type? Reusing `GitPoller` is confusing (it's not git). Creating a new `CronPoller` interface with identical methods is a DRY violation.

The fix is trivial: rename to a generic `Poller` interface, export it from triggers.ts, and use it for both. The `GitPoller` type alias can stay for backward compat.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose A — clean naming, minimal diff (add type alias, update exports). DRY is important per your preferences. Completeness: A=9/10, B=7/10.

options.label: A) Generic Poller interface (Recommended)
options.description: Rename to `Poller { start(); stop(); }`. Add `type GitPoller = Poller` alias for backward compat. ~5 LOC change. (human: ~15 min / CC: ~2 min)

options.label: B) Keep GitPoller, duplicate as CronPoller
options.description: Create identical CronPoller interface. Two interfaces with same shape. Simple but DRY violation. (human: ~10 min / CC: ~2 min)

decisions.rationale: Renaming to a generic Poller interface with a GitPoller type alias eliminates duplication while preserving backward compat — textbook DRY with a ~5 LOC diff.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw Phase 4b on main. CODE QUALITY ISSUE 1: atomicWriteJSON reconciliation with checkpoint.ts.

The plan proposes extracting a shared atomicWriteJSON() utility and says checkpoint.ts will reuse it. But the existing checkpoint.ts writeCheckpoint() (lines 24-47) uses a DIFFERENT pattern:
- Random hex in tmp filename: `checkpoint.tmp.${randomBytes(4).toString('hex')}.json`
- Different backup naming: `checkpoint.prev.json` (not `.bak`)

The plan's atomicWriteJSON uses `.tmp` suffix and `.bak` suffix. These are incompatible — migrating checkpoint.ts to the shared utility would change the backup file naming from `.prev.json` to `.bak`, breaking any code that looks for `checkpoint.prev.json`.

The random hex in checkpoint.ts was defensive (collision safety), but since we're single-threaded it's unnecessary.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose B — the shared utility is still valuable for job-runner + cron-state, and we get DRY for those two. Migrating checkpoint.ts requires updating readCheckpoint() fallback path too — extra diff for no functional gain. Completeness: A=8/10, B=9/10.

options.label: A) Migrate checkpoint.ts to shared utility
options.description: Change checkpoint.ts to use atomicWriteJSON. Must also update readCheckpoint fallback to look for .bak instead of .prev.json. Broader diff, full DRY. (human: ~2 hours / CC: ~10 min)

options.label: B) Shared utility for new code only (Recommended)
options.description: atomicWriteJSON used by job-runner.ts + cron-state. checkpoint.ts keeps its existing pattern. Avoids touching a working module. Minimal diff. (human: ~1 hour / CC: ~5 min)

options.label: C) Skip shared utility entirely
options.description: Inline atomic write in each module. Duplicated ~8 lines in 2 places. Simpler but DRY violation. (human: ~30 min / CC: ~3 min)

decisions.chosen: B) Shared utility for new code only (Recommended)
decisions.rationale: DRY is satisfied for the new code, and migrating checkpoint.ts risks breaking a working module for zero functional gain — explicit over clever says don't refactor what works.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw Phase 4b on main. CODE QUALITY ISSUE 2: validateDaemonConfig hardcodes 'git_poll' rejection.

Line 71 of daemon.ts: `if (t.type !== "git_poll") return \`triggers[${i}].type must be \"git_poll\"\`;`

This must change to accept both 'git_poll' and 'cron' trigger types with type-specific validation (different required fields for each). The existing test on line 95-97 explicitly tests that type='cron' is REJECTED — this test will need updating to pass.

This is a straightforward fix with no real alternatives — I'll note it as a required change rather than waste a question. The validation should switch on trigger type:
- `git_poll`: validate intervalSeconds, skills, optional debounceSeconds/branch
- `cron`: validate expression (via cron-parser), skills, optional catchUp/timezone

Moving on.

options.label: A) Acknowledged — proceed (Recommended)
options.description: Required change with obvious implementation. Update validation to switch on trigger type + update test. No decision needed.

options.label: B) Discuss further
options.description: Want to discuss the validation approach in more detail.

decisions.rationale: Straightforward required change with obvious implementation — no real decision to make, just do it.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw Phase 4b on main. TEST ISSUE 1: The cron poller needs dependency injection for cron-parser.

The plan says cron poller uses `cron.parseExpression(expr, { tz })` directly. But to test catch-up semantics (lastRun 48h ago, DST transitions, etc.), you need to control what prev() and next() return. Importing cron-parser directly makes unit tests depend on real time.

The git poller solved this elegantly: `GitPollerDeps` injects `getHead`, `setInterval`, `setTimeout`, etc. The cron poller needs similar DI.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose A — follows the existing git poller pattern exactly. DI for time functions + cron parsing gives deterministic tests. Your preference for well-tested code is non-negotiable, and you can't test catch-up properly without controlling time. Completeness: A=10/10, B=5/10.

options.label: A) CronPollerDeps with mock support (Recommended)
options.description: Inject: parseExpression (or a wrapper), Date.now, setInterval/clearInterval. Same pattern as GitPollerDeps. Enables deterministic catch-up + DST tests. (human: ~2 hours / CC: ~10 min)

options.label: B) Use vi.useFakeTimers only
options.description: Vitest fake timers for setInterval + manual Date mocking via vi.setSystemTime. No DI needed. But cron-parser's prev()/next() use internal Date calls that may not respect fake timers. Fragile. (human: ~1 hour / CC: ~8 min)

decisions.rationale: Follows the established GitPollerDeps pattern exactly, guaranteeing deterministic tests for catch-up and DST without fragile fake-timer hacks.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw Phase 4b on main. TEST ISSUE 2: Config reload testing requires a testable extraction.

The config reload logic (mtime polling, diff-based restart, orphan pruning) is planned to live inside `startDaemon()` in daemon.ts. But `startDaemon()` is the full daemon lifecycle function — it forks processes, opens sockets, writes PID files. You can't unit test config reload without spinning up an actual daemon.

The existing codebase solves this pattern well: `buildIPCHandler()` is extracted as a pure function that takes dependencies and returns a handler. Config reload needs the same treatment.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose A — extract the reload logic as a testable function. This is the existing pattern (buildIPCHandler, createJobRunner, createGitPoller all follow it). Explicit > clever. Completeness: A=10/10, B=4/10.

options.label: A) Extract reloadConfig() function (Recommended)
options.description: Extract a pure function: reloadConfig(oldConfig, newConfig, pollers) that returns { toStop, toStart, toKeep, prunedKeys }. Unit-testable with no daemon lifecycle. (human: ~2 hours / CC: ~10 min)

options.label: B) Test via integration only
options.description: Write the reload logic inline in startDaemon, test by actually starting a daemon in tests. Slow, flaky, hard to test edge cases like invalid JSON. (human: ~3 hours / CC: ~20 min)

decisions.rationale: This follows the established codebase pattern (buildIPCHandler, createJobRunner, createGitPoller) and makes config reload fully unit-testable without daemon lifecycle dependencies.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw Phase 4b on main. TODO PROPOSAL 1: Config reload integration test.

The plan has unit tests for reloadConfig() (extracted function), but no test that verifies the full mtime-polling loop: daemon detects file change → reads config → calls reloadConfig() → pollers actually restart. This is the integration seam.

A lightweight integration test (mock fs.statSync for mtime, mock config file reads) would catch wiring bugs between the mtime polling loop and the reload function. Not needed for Phase 4b MVP but valuable for Phase 4c hardening.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose A — small TODO for Phase 4c. The unit tests cover the logic; this catches wiring. Completeness: A=8/10, B=6/10, C=9/10.

options.label: A) Add to TODOS.md (Recommended)
options.description: P3 TODO for Phase 4c hardening. Integration test for mtime poll → reload → poller restart. (human: ~2 hours / CC: ~15 min)

options.label: B) Skip
options.description: Unit tests for reloadConfig() are sufficient. Integration test adds complexity for marginal value.

options.label: C) Build it now
options.description: Write the integration test as part of Phase 4b. Highest coverage. (human: ~3 hours / CC: ~15 min)

decisions.rationale: Config reload is squarely in Phase 4b's blast radius and CC effort is ~15 min — P2 auto-approves in-blast-radius expansions under 1 day, and deferring a 15-min task creates more overhead than just doing it.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw Phase 4b on main. TODO PROPOSAL 2: Update TODOS.md — Phase 4 daemon entry is stale.

The current TODOS.md has a P2 entry for 'Daemon Mode (Phase 4)' that describes Phase 4a work which is already COMPLETE. It should be updated to reflect Phase 4b (scheduling) as the current work, and Phase 4c (hardening) as the next deferred item. The existing P3 entries (Codebase Summary, Adaptive maxTurns) are still valid.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose A — stale TODOs are worse than no TODOs. Quick cleanup. Completeness: A=9/10, B=5/10.

options.label: A) Update TODOS.md (Recommended)
options.description: Replace stale Phase 4 entry with Phase 4c hardening TODO. Mark Phase 4a+4b as complete in the entry. ~5 min.

options.label: B) Skip
options.description: Leave as-is. The entry is technically wrong but doesn't block anything.

decisions.rationale: Stale documentation is tech debt — quick 5-min cleanup keeps the project state accurate, consistent with the 'boil lakes' pattern of fixing everything in blast radius.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw Phase 4b on main. TODO PROPOSAL 3: CLI schedule output format.

The plan defines the IPC response structure for 'schedule' but doesn't specify how the CLI formats the output. Should it be a table? JSON? Colored text? This is a taste decision.

The existing CLI subcommands use ANSI-colored text (status shows key-value pairs, log shows raw text). A simple table matching that style would be consistent.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose A — matches existing CLI style. Completeness: A=9/10, B=7/10.

options.label: A) ANSI table like status (Recommended)
options.description: Colored key-value display per trigger, with next fire time highlighted. Matches existing daemon status output. (human: ~1 hour / CC: ~5 min)

options.label: B) JSON output
options.description: Raw JSON dump for machine consumption. Simple but not human-friendly. Could add --json flag later.

decisions.rationale: Matches the existing CLI style (status, log) so users get a consistent experience, and a --json flag can always be added later.

decisions.question:
GaryClaw Phase 4b on main. All review sections are complete. Want an outside voice? A different AI system can give a brutally honest, independent challenge of this plan — logical gaps, feasibility risks, and blind spots that are hard to catch from inside the review. Takes about 2 minutes.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose A — an independent second opinion catches structural blind spots. Two different AI models agreeing on a plan is stronger signal than one model's thorough review. Completeness: A=9/10, B=7/10.

options.label: A) Get the outside voice (Recommended)
options.description: Claude subagent gives a brutally honest, independent challenge. ~2 minutes.

options.label: B) Skip
options.description: Proceed to outputs. Review is thorough enough.

decisions.rationale: The review is already thorough with multiple test issues and TODOs addressed — another review cycle is stale deliberation when we should be building.
